_comment: Aperio exam fixture — import via POST /api/memories/import (the API reads only the `memories` array; this _comment field is ignored). Every entry carries the tag `aperio-exam` so the whole set can be filtered and bulk-deleted after testing. Persona: Maya Chen, a fictional staff backend engineer, with two projects (Nimbus, Beacon) so recall/wiki/dedup have coherent clusters to work on. See exam.md for the prompts that exercise this data.

memories.title: Maya Chen — staff backend engineer
memories.content: Maya Chen is a staff backend engineer who works fully remote from Lisbon, Portugal. She leads the platform team and is the primary owner of the Nimbus pricing service. Reachable as @maya on the team chat.

memories.title: Maya's primary languages are Go and Rust
memories.content: Maya writes Go as her primary backend language and reaches for Rust for performance-critical components. She avoids Python for anything beyond quick scripts.

memories.title: Maya's working hours and timezone
memories.content: Maya is based in Lisbon (WET/WEST) and keeps roughly 10:00–18:00 local hours. She dislikes synchronous meetings before 11:00.

memories.title: Tabs over spaces, 100-column limit
memories.content: Maya prefers tabs for indentation, never spaces, and wraps code at a 100-column soft limit. gofmt settles most of this for Go, but the rule applies to all languages.

memories.title: Indentation: tabs, not spaces
memories.content: Maya always uses tab characters for indentation across every project and considers space-indentation a code-review blocker. (Deliberate near-duplicate of the formatting preference for dedup testing.)

memories.title: Conventional Commits, imperative mood
memories.content: Maya requires Conventional Commits (feat:, fix:, chore:, refactor:) with an imperative-mood subject under 72 characters. Bodies explain the why, not the what.

memories.title: Table-driven tests in Go
memories.content: Maya writes Go tests as table-driven cases with subtests via t.Run, and expects new code to ship with them. She favors the standard library testing package over third-party assertion frameworks.

memories.title: Catppuccin Mocha, dark mode everywhere
memories.content: Maya runs dark mode in every tool and uses the Catppuccin Mocha palette in her editor, terminal, and dashboards.

memories.title: Oat-milk flat white, no sugar
memories.content: Maya's standing coffee order is an oat-milk flat white with no sugar. She drinks two a day and none after 15:00.

memories.title: Nimbus — real-time pricing service
memories.content: Nimbus is the team's real-time pricing service. It computes quote prices on demand for the storefront, must answer under 50ms p99, and is the highest-traffic service the platform team owns. Maya is the primary owner.

memories.title: Nimbus uses NATS for events, not Kafka
memories.content: The team chose NATS JetStream over Kafka for Nimbus's event bus. Rationale: far simpler ops for the throughput Nimbus needs, single binary, and the team had no existing Kafka expertise. Revisit only if cross-region replication becomes a hard requirement.

memories.title: Nimbus data stores: Postgres + Redis
memories.content: Nimbus uses Postgres as the system of record for price rules and Redis as a read-through cache for computed quotes with a 5-second TTL. The cache is treated as disposable; Postgres is authoritative.

memories.title: Nimbus deploys on Fly.io with blue-green
memories.content: Nimbus deploys to Fly.io across three regions using a blue-green strategy. Rollouts gate on a synthetic pricing check before traffic shifts. No canary; the blue-green cutover plus the synthetic check was deemed enough.

memories.title: Fixed N+1 query in Nimbus pricing endpoint
memories.content: The /quote endpoint issued one DB round-trip per line item. Fixed by batching rule lookups through a single IN query keyed on SKU, then assembling in memory. p99 dropped from 180ms to 38ms. The batched loader lives in pricing/ruleloader.go.

memories.title: Tamed the Redis connection storm in Nimbus
memories.content: Under deploy churn, Nimbus opened thousands of Redis connections and tipped over. Fix: cap the pool at 50 connections per instance and add a 200ms dial timeout with jittered backoff. Connection count is now flat across rollouts.

memories.title: Nimbus architecture doc (Notion)
memories.content: The living architecture doc for Nimbus is in Notion: https://www.notion.so/team/nimbus-architecture. It covers the event flow, cache strategy, and the deploy gates. Treat it as the canonical design reference.

memories.title: Beacon — iOS companion app
memories.content: Beacon is the iOS companion app that surfaces Nimbus pricing alerts to field reps. It is owned by the mobile team; Maya consults on the pricing API contract but does not own the app.

memories.title: Beacon is SwiftUI, minimum iOS 16
memories.content: Beacon is built in SwiftUI and targets a minimum of iOS 16 so it can rely on the newer Swift Concurrency and NavigationStack APIs. UIKit is used only for a couple of legacy chart wrappers.

memories.title: Go style guide the team follows
memories.content: The team's Go conventions follow Google's Go Style Guide: https://google.github.io/styleguide/go/. Local additions (error wrapping, package layout) are appended in the Nimbus repo's CONTRIBUTING.md.

memories.title: NATS JetStream tuning post
memories.content: Reference Maya keeps for JetStream consumer tuning: https://nats.io/blog/jetstream-consumer-tuning. Used when sizing Nimbus's durable consumers.

memories.title: Maya tends to over-build caching layers
memories.content: Across the Nimbus and a prior project, Maya reaches for a cache early and sometimes before profiling shows it's needed. Worth gently asking 'have we measured this?' before adding another cache tier.

memories.title: Maya prefers async-first communication
memories.content: Inferred from her stated dislike of early meetings and her detailed written design docs: Maya gets more done with async, written communication than with synchronous calls. Default to a written summary over scheduling a call.

memories.title: Nimbus on-call rotation is weekly
memories.content: The platform team runs a weekly Nimbus on-call rotation starting Mondays. Maya covers roughly one week in four. Pages route through the team's incident tool.

memories.title: Nimbus SLO: 50ms p99, 99.95% availability
memories.content: Nimbus's agreed SLOs are 50ms p99 latency on /quote and 99.95% monthly availability. Breaching the latency SLO for two consecutive weeks triggers a reliability-focused sprint.

memories.title: Maya owns the pricing API contract
memories.content: Maya is the decision-maker for the Nimbus pricing API contract (the OpenAPI spec consumed by the storefront and by Beacon). Breaking changes go through her review.
